fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a command-line tool for working with JWTs. Developers keep complaining they're spending too much time hand-rolling token stuff in bash scripts and it's causing bugs in staging. Basically the tool should let you create tokens and also read them back / check if they're legit. A few things I know we need: it should handle the usual token fields like who issued it, who it's for, expiry, etc., plus let you throw in your own custom fields including numbers, booleans, and nested JSON objects (not just strings). For the time stuff, people don't want to calculate Unix timestamps by hand — they want to say something like 'expires in 10 minutes' and have it just work. Also we need it to work with key files on disk, not just inline secrets, because some of our infra uses RSA keys. Oh and it should behave like that validation approach we settled on for the auth service — where bad inputs give back a clean normalized error code instead of a raw exception dump. The test runner should output files that map to each case so we can diff them. I don't have all the exact flag names sorted out, refer to what the team already standardized for the encoding/decoding options. Should be well-structured code, not a single giant file. Let me know if you have questions.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. For custom claims, we do want people passing them with the -P flag in the format key=value. The value should get parsed as JSON if it looks like a number, boolean, array, or object, so examples here are exactly -P number=10, -P array=[1, 2, 3], -P object={"foo": "bar"}. If it doesn’t look like one of those, it stays a string. Also, if the value does not contain exactly one = separator, return error=invalid_payload_item.

On the time side, relative durations should be accepted as strings like '+10 min -30 sec' or '+5 min'. We do want to support multiple additive/subtractive terms, and the offset is calculated from the automatically generated iat (issue time). If the string can’t be parsed as either an integer Unix timestamp or a valid relative duration, return error=invalid_timestamp_or_duration. Also confirming the encoder adds an iat (issued-at) claim by default, and you can suppress that with --no-iat. One subtle thing there: expiration via --exp without an explicit timestamp should still be set relative to the current time even when --no-iat is used, and validation should still succeed if the exp claim is present.

For keys on disk, the way to do it is prefixing the -S argument value with @, for example -S @./tests/private_rsa_key.der. That file is read as raw bytes. Same behavior for HMAC secrets and asymmetric keys (RSA, ECDSA). For RSA specifically, sign with the private key file and verify with the public key file, both in DER format.

One more decode behavior detail: if a token is decoded with a mismatching secret, the output should be exactly status=invalid\nerror=invalid_signature\n followed by the decoded header and claim fields that were requested in the report. The payload fields still get decoded and rendered even when the signature is invalid. If no secret is provided at all, treat it as unverified and return status=valid.

And just to restate the validation piece in the same spirit as the original ask, feature 6 input validation errors are invalid_payload_item for malformed -P values and invalid_timestamp_or_duration for unrecognized -e/-n values, and those should come back as plain error=<code> lines with no exception stack traces.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fn encode(args: EncodeArgs) -> Result<String, JwtError>; fn decode(args: DecodeArgs) -> Result<DecodeOutput, JwtError>

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes one file per case to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt, e.g. rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_encode_mixed_claims@000.txt. The --cases-dir argument defaults to public_test_cases and namespaces output so different runs never overwrite each other. Each file contains only the raw stdout from the adapter, no PASS/FAIL metadata.

trigger_keywords: leading trailing spaces in token input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The decode path must trim leading and trailing whitespace from the token string before attempting to parse or verify it. This ensures tokens copied from terminals or editors with accidental surrounding whitespace are handled cleanly.

Array claim values are rendered in compact JSON form without spaces, e.g. [1,2,3] not [1, 2, 3]. Object nested values accessed via dot notation (e.g. claim.object.foo) render just the leaf string value, e.g. bar.

trigger_keywords: how to report iat without exact value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a report field ends with .presence (e.g. claim.iat.presence), the output line uses the base key name and prints either 'present' or 'absent' depending on whether that claim exists in the decoded payload. For example, claim.iat.presence renders as either claim.iat=present or claim.iat=absent.

trigger_keywords: should I use one file or separate modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-file structure (e.g. src/, tests/) reflecting distinct responsibilities: argument parsing, core JWT logic, I/O adapter, error types, and output formatting. The execution adapter that reads JSON commands from stdin must be physically separated from the core domain logic. A single-file god-file approach is explicitly prohibited for this project.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'encode_then_decode', 'decode', 'encode_error'. Each value maps to one feature.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 encode mixed claims
show me feature1 encode mixed claims test cases
what are the feature1 encode mixed claims test inputs
give me the feature1_encode_mixed_claims test file
feature1 encode mixed claims expected output
what does feature1 encode mixed claims expect
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature1_encode_mixed_claims:
{
  "description": "Encode a signed token from registered claims, typed custom claims, and a JSON object payload, then decode it to verify the externally visible header and payload fields.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "action": "encode_then_decode",
        "encode_args": [
          "encode",
          "-S",
          "1234567890",
          "-A",
          "HS256",
          "-a",
          "yolo",
          "-e",
          "1893456000",
          "-i",
          "yolo-service",
          "-k",
          "1234",
          "-n",
          "1542492313",
          "--jti",
          "yolo-jti",
          "-P",
          "this=that",
          "-P",
          "number=10",
          "-P",
          "array=[1, 2, 3]",
          "-P",
          "object={\"foo\": \"bar\"}",
          "-s",
          "yolo-subject",
          "{\"test\":\"json value\",\"bool\":true,\"json_number\":1}"
        ],
        "decode_args": [
          "decode",
          "-S",
          "1234567890",
          "-A",
          "HS256"
        ],
        "report": [
          "status",
          "header.alg",
          "header.kid",
          "claim.aud",
          "claim.iss",
          "claim.sub",
          "claim.nbf",
          "claim.exp",
          "claim.jti",
          "claim.this",
          "claim.test",
          "claim.bool",
          "claim.json_number",
          "claim.number",
          "claim.array",
          "claim.object.foo",
          "claim.iat.presence"
        ]
      },
      "expected_output": "status=valid\nheader.alg=HS256\nheader.kid=1234\nclaim.aud=yolo\nclaim.iss=yolo-service\nclaim.sub=yolo-subject\nclaim.nbf=1542492313\nclaim.exp=1893456000\nclaim.jti=yolo-jti\nclaim.this=that\nclaim.test=json value\nclaim.bool=true\nclaim.json_number=1\nclaim.number=10\nclaim.array=[1,2,3]\nclaim.object.foo=bar\nclaim.iat=present\n"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to what the team already standardized for the encoding/decoding options
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The CLI flag conventions defined in the test cases: -S for secret, -A for algorithm, -a for audience, -e for expiration, -i for issuer, -k for key id, -n for not-before, -s for subject, --jti for JWT ID, -P for custom payload claims, --exp for auto-expiration, --no-iat to suppress issued-at

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have fully spec'd out on my end — you'd need to dig into the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or check with the engineering lead who owns the auth token standards. I don't want to give you wrong info on something this specific.

feature1_encode_mixed_claims.json.description: Encode a signed token from registered claims, typed custom claims, and a JSON object payload, then decode it to verify the externally visible header and payload fields.

header.alg=HS256

claim.aud=yolo

claim.sub=yolo-subject

claim.json_number=1
claim.number=10
claim.array=[1,2,3]
claim.object.foo=bar

feature2_automatic_time_claims.json.description: Encode tokens using automatic time-claim behavior and render whether issue-time and expiration claims are present after decoding.

feature3_relative_time_expressions.json.description: Encode tokens with relative duration expressions for time-based claims and render the decoded second offsets from the generated issue time.

feature4_decode_existing_tokens.json.description: Decode existing token strings under verified, unverified, whitespace-trimmed, and invalid-signature conditions and render observable status plus selected fields.

cases.expected_output:
status=valid
header.alg=HS256
header.typ=JWT
claim.exp=1893456000
claim.iat=1542492313
claim.this=that

feature5_key_files.json.description: Encode tokens using key material loaded from files and verify the produced tokens with the matching file-backed verification input.

feature6_input_validation_errors.json.description: Attempt to encode tokens with invalid custom-claim and timestamp arguments and render normalized input-validation errors.
